title: Calcolatore di Calorie Giornaliere
description: Calcola il tuo fabbisogno calorico giornaliero per perdere peso, mantenere il peso attuale o aumentare di peso. Basato sull'equazione Mifflin-St Jeor.

faq.title: Domande Frequenti
faq.q1: Quante calorie dovrei assumere per perdere peso?
faq.a1: L'approccio sicuro e basato su evidenze è un deficit di 300-500 kcal al giorno sotto il TDEE. Questo produce una perdita di grasso di 0,3-0,5 kg a settimana. Deficit maggiori (500-750 kcal) accelerano la perdita ma rischiano di ridurre la massa muscolare e l'adattamento metabolico. Non scendere mai sotto il BMR con l'apporto calorico.
faq.q2: Cos'è il BMR e come differisce dal TDEE?
faq.a2: Il BMR (Metabolismo Basale) è l'energia che il corpo brucia a completo riposo. Il TDEE (Dispendio Energetico Giornaliero Totale) aggiunge le calorie bruciate attraverso tutte le attività fisiche più l'effetto termico del cibo. Il TDEE è sempre più alto del BMR — tipicamente 1,2-1,9× a seconda del livello di attività.
faq.q3: Cos'è l'equazione Mifflin-St Jeor e perché è la migliore formula?
faq.a3: L'equazione Mifflin-St Jeor, pubblicata nel 1990 e validata dall'Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics, è la formula predittiva più accurata per il BMR negli adulti non obesi. Una revisione del 2005 ha trovato che Mifflin-St Jeor è più accurata nell'82% dei casi testati.
faq.q4: Quante calorie giornaliere sono sicure per la perdita di peso?
faq.a4: Per la maggior parte degli adulti, 1.200-1.500 kcal/giorno (donne) o 1.500-1.800 kcal/giorno (uomini) sono i minimi pratici sotto supervisione medica. Scendere al di sotto di questi livelli richiede supervisione clinica. L'approccio più sicuro e sostenibile è creare un deficit del 15-20% sotto il TDEE.
faq.q5: I macronutrienti influenzano la perdita di peso oltre alle calorie?
faq.a5: Le calorie totali sono il principale determinante del cambiamento di peso a lungo termine. Tuttavia, i macronutrienti influenzano la sazietà, la composizione corporea e la facilità di aderenza. Le proteine (1,6-2,2 g/kg di peso corporeo) sono le più sazianti e proteggono la massa muscolare durante la perdita di peso.
faq.q6: Come cambio il mio obiettivo calorico quando smetto di perdere peso?
faq.a6: Quando raggiungi il tuo obiettivo di peso, aumenta gradualmente l'apporto calorico (100-200 kcal a settimana) fino a che il peso si stabilizza. Questo è il tuo nuovo TDEE al peso target. Il TDEE cambia con il peso corporeo — ogni 5 kg in meno riducono il TDEE di circa 50-75 kcal/giorno.
faq.q7: Le calorie delle bevande contano nella gestione del peso?
faq.a7: Assolutamente. Le bevande caloriche — succhi di frutta, bevande zuccherate, alcolici, caffè con sciroppi — possono aggiungere 200-500 kcal/giorno senza contribuire alla sazietà. L'alcol fornisce 7 kcal/grammo. Per la gestione del peso, dai la priorità all'acqua, tè e caffè non zuccherati.
faq.q8: Come posso mantenere la perdita di peso a lungo termine?
faq.a8: I fattori predittivi del mantenimento a lungo termine includono: monitoraggio regolare del peso, colazione regolare, dieta coerente nei giorni festivi, alto livello di attività fisica, e automonitoraggio continuo delle calorie. Studi mostrano che chi monitora regolarmente il peso mantiene meglio la perdita.

content.whatIs:
The calorie calculator estimates how many calories you need each day to fuel your body and reach your health goals. It uses the Mifflin-St Jeor equation — the most accurate predictive formula for Basal Metabolic Rate recommended by the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics — to establish your baseline energy expenditure, then applies your activity level multiplier to calculate your Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE). From there, it generates personalized calorie targets for weight loss, weight maintenance, or muscle gain, along with a recommended macro breakdown for protein, fat, and carbohydrates.

Calorie needs are not one-size-fits-all. A sedentary 55-year-old woman weighing 60 kg and an active 25-year-old man weighing 90 kg have maintenance calorie needs that differ by over 1,500 kcal per day. Generic dietary advice ('eat 1,200 calories to lose weight') is not just imprecise — it is potentially harmful. For the heavier active man, 1,200 kcal is far below BMR and will trigger muscle loss and metabolic adaptation. For the smaller sedentary woman, 1,200 kcal may be close to maintenance. Individual calculation is the only accurate starting point.

This calculator produces five outputs: your BMR, your maintenance calories (TDEE), three weight-loss deficit targets (mild, standard, extreme), two weight-gain targets (mild, standard), and a macro breakdown for your selected goal. These numbers are evidence-based estimates with approximately 10% individual variation — they are starting points to calibrate over 2–4 weeks by tracking actual weight change versus predicted change.
content.formula:
Mifflin-St Jeor BMR Equations:
Men:   BMR = (10 × weight_kg) + (6.25 × height_cm) − (5 × age) + 5
Women: BMR = (10 × weight_kg) + (6.25 × height_cm) − (5 × age) − 161

TDEE (Maintenance) = BMR × Activity Multiplier:
  Sedentary:       BMR × 1.200  (desk job, little exercise)
  Lightly Active:  BMR × 1.375  (exercise 1–3 days/week)
  Moderately Active: BMR × 1.550 (exercise 3–5 days/week)
  Very Active:     BMR × 1.725  (hard exercise 6–7 days/week)
  Extra Active:    BMR × 1.900  (physical job + hard daily training)

Calorie Targets from TDEE:
  Mild loss (−0.25 kg/week):    TDEE − 250 kcal
  Standard loss (−0.5 kg/week): TDEE − 500 kcal
  Extreme loss (−1 kg/week):    TDEE − 1,000 kcal
  Mild gain (+0.25 kg/week):    TDEE + 250 kcal
  Standard gain (+0.5 kg/week): TDEE + 500 kcal

Recommended Macros (per goal calorie target):
  Protein: 2.0g per kg body weight (4 kcal/g)
  Fat:     25% of total calories (9 kcal/g)
  Carbs:   remaining calories (4 kcal/g)
content.example:
Example 1 — Weight loss for a woman:
32-year-old woman, 168 cm, 72 kg, moderately active.
BMR = (10×72) + (6.25×168) − (5×32) − 161 = 720 + 1,050 − 160 − 161 = 1,449 kcal
TDEE = 1,449 × 1.55 = 2,246 kcal/day (maintenance)
Standard loss target: 2,246 − 500 = 1,746 kcal/day → projected −0.5 kg/week
Macros: Protein 144g (576 kcal), Fat 49g (436 kcal), Carbs 184g (734 kcal)

Example 2 — Muscle gain for a man:
27-year-old man, 180 cm, 78 kg, very active (lifts 6×/week).
BMR = (10×78) + (6.25×180) − (5×27) + 5 = 780 + 1,125 − 135 + 5 = 1,775 kcal
TDEE = 1,775 × 1.725 = 3,062 kcal/day (maintenance)
Mild gain target: 3,062 + 250 = 3,312 kcal/day → lean bulk
Macros: Protein 156g (624 kcal), Fat 92g (828 kcal), Carbs 465g (1,860 kcal)

Example 3 — Maintenance for a sedentary older man:
55-year-old man, 175 cm, 83 kg, sedentary.
BMR = (10×83) + (6.25×175) − (5×55) + 5 = 1,797 kcal
TDEE = 1,797 × 1.200 = 2,156 kcal/day
content.howItWorks_1: Enter your weight, height, age, and biological sex. These four values feed directly into the Mifflin-St Jeor equation to calculate your BMR. Select your unit system (metric or imperial) — the calculator converts all values internally to kg and cm before running the formula.
content.howItWorks_2: Choose your activity level honestly. This is the most error-prone step — most people overestimate their activity level by one category. 'Sedentary' is correct even if you exercise 3× per week, if you sit for 8+ hours per day at a desk job. The activity multiplier is a major factor in the final calorie target, so accurate selection matters significantly.
content.howItWorks_3: Select your goal (lose weight, maintain, or gain muscle) and click Calculate. The calculator displays your BMR, maintenance calories, all deficit and surplus targets with their projected weekly weight change rates, and a macro breakdown (protein, fat, carbohydrates in grams per day) for your selected goal calorie target.
content.whyItMatters:
Calorie needs are the mathematical foundation of every evidence-based nutrition approach. Without knowing your individual maintenance calorie requirement, you cannot create a meaningful fat loss deficit or an appropriate muscle-building surplus. This is why generic diet plans fail so frequently: a plan that works perfectly for one person is systematically wrong for everyone with different size, age, and activity level. Personalized calorie calculation is not optional for effective nutrition — it is the starting point.

The consequences of eating significantly below BMR deserve special attention. When calorie intake drops far below BMR, the body enters a survival mode called metabolic adaptation: it reduces non-exercise movement, lowers body temperature, decreases thyroid output, and reduces BMR itself by 10–25%. This is the biological mechanism behind the well-documented 'weight loss plateau' that most dieters experience after initial rapid loss. A 2016 study in the journal Obesity tracking Biggest Loser contestants six years after the competition found that competitors' metabolisms had decreased by an average of 704 kcal/day compared to what would be predicted for their new body weight — and these metabolic deficits persisted years later.

On the muscle-building side, calorie surplus is equally important and equally misunderstood. Without sufficient calorie surplus, the body cannot build new muscle tissue regardless of how much protein is consumed or how effectively training is performed. Protein synthesis requires energy above and beyond maintenance. However, the common 'bulk' approach of eating at a large surplus (1,000+ kcal above TDEE) accelerates fat gain without proportionally accelerating muscle growth — because the muscle protein synthesis rate is capped by hormonal and physiological limits. The optimal muscle-building surplus is just 200–500 kcal above TDEE, not the large surpluses popularized in fitness culture.
content.limitations:
The Mifflin-St Jeor equation estimates BMR with a mean error of approximately 10%. This means your actual BMR could be 150–200 kcal higher or lower than calculated, even if all inputs are entered perfectly. Individual variation comes from genetics, hormonal status, medical conditions, medications, and body composition. People with higher muscle mass than average will have a higher actual BMR than predicted; those with hypothyroidism may have a 15–30% lower actual BMR.

The activity multipliers are population averages and are frequently the largest source of estimation error. Research shows that people consistently overestimate both exercise duration and intensity, and underestimate sedentary time. Wearable fitness trackers often overestimate calorie burn by 15–40%. For the most accurate TDEE estimate, track your food intake at maintenance level for 2–3 weeks and observe actual weight change — if you gain weight eating 2,000 kcal, your actual TDEE is below 2,000; if you lose weight, it is above.

This calculator does not account for medical conditions that significantly alter metabolism: hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), Cushing's syndrome, or insulin resistance all affect calorie needs in ways the equation cannot predict. If you are following calorie targets from this calculator and experiencing unexpected results (no weight change, unexplained fatigue, or excessive weight loss), consult a physician to rule out metabolic conditions.
content.tips_1: Track your actual food intake for the first 2–4 weeks using a calorie-tracking app (Cronometer, MyFitnessPal) and compare predicted to actual weight change. If you are eating at your calculated loss target but not losing weight, your TDEE estimate is likely too high — reduce by 100–150 kcal and recheck after 2 more weeks. This calibration approach produces more accurate long-term results than any formula alone.
content.tips_2: Prioritize protein at every meal, especially during calorie restriction. The minimum threshold for preserving muscle while losing fat is 1.6g of protein per kg of body weight per day — higher is better, up to about 2.4g/kg for athletes. Protein is also more satiating than carbs or fat per calorie, making it easier to maintain a deficit without constant hunger. Practical target: aim for 25–40g of protein per meal.
content.tips_3: Do not use 'extreme loss' calorie targets (−1,000 kcal/day) for more than 1–2 weeks at a time without professional supervision. While rapid initial weight loss feels motivating, sustained extreme deficits cause disproportionate muscle loss, significant metabolic adaptation, nutrient deficiencies, and poor workout performance. Moderate deficits of 300–500 kcal sustained consistently produce better long-term body composition outcomes than aggressive short-term restriction.
content.tips_4: Recalculate your calorie needs every 4–6 weeks during active weight loss or gain phases. As your body weight changes, so does your BMR and TDEE. A person who has lost 10 kg needs fewer calories to maintain their new weight than they did at the start. Failing to adjust calorie targets as weight changes is one of the most common reasons fat loss plateaus — the old target is no longer a deficit relative to the new, lower TDEE.
